verses.commentary: The turning point. God remembered (zakar, זָכַר) means to act with purpose and covenant faithfulness, not that God had forgotten. The wind (ruach, רוּחַ) is the same word as Spirit in Genesis 1:2. The Spirit that hovered over chaos now brings order again. Re-creation begins.
The sources of flood are shut. Waters above and below are separated again, reversing the deluge. Creation's order returns.
Gradual return to order. The same timespan as the rising (7:24). Judgment and deliverance are measured. 150 days up, 150 days down.
Solid ground appears. The ark finds rest on Ararat (modern Turkey region). The floating ends; stability returns.
Progressive revelation. More and more land appears. Hope grows as mountaintops become visible.
Forty days again, echoing the forty days of rain (7:12). Noah acts. He investigates the outside world.
The raven, a scavenger, can survive in harsh conditions. It finds enough to sustain itself. The test is inconclusive.
The dove is more delicate, needs suitable habitat. Better indicator of livable conditions than the raven.
Not yet ready. The dove returns. Noah extends his hand, a tender image. He provides refuge until the time is right.
Patience. Seven day intervals suggest a pattern of completeness. Noah waits and tests again.
The olive leaf is proof of life. Vegetation has returned. The earth is renewing. The first sign of hope, a symbol of peace ever since.
The dove finds a home. The earth is ready. The third sending succeeds. Noah understands the sign.
A new year. Noah is now 601. He opens the ark and sees dry land. Vision returns. The world is visible again.
Full restoration. Exactly one year and ten days after entering the ark (compare 7:11). A full cycle of time. The earth is ready for habitation.
God gives the command to exit, just as he commanded entry (7:1). Human action follows divine initiative.
The creation mandate is renewed. Be fruitful and multiply (1:28) is spoken again. A new beginning for all living creatures.
The family exits. They step into a cleansed world. Eight people to restart humanity.
Orderly exit. The procession reverses the entrance. Creation emerges from the ark in the same organized way it entered.
First act: worship. Noah's response to salvation is sacrifice. The clean animals taken in sevens (7:2) now have purpose. Worship requires cost.

God receives the sacrifice favorably. The pleasing aroma (nichoach, נִיחוֹחַ) suggests satisfaction and acceptance. Remarkably, God acknowledges that human nature has not changed (every inclination is evil), yet he promises restraint. Grace does not wait for human perfection.

God commits to cosmic stability. The rhythms of creation will continue. Seasons, agriculture, time itself are guaranteed. Despite human sin, God will sustain the world. This is covenant faithfulness to the created order.
